humanities_role_explanation: Humanities subjects (including History, Philosophy, Music, and Visual Arts) are utilized as topical content categories to test factual accuracy in long-form generation. However, from a humanist perspective, these subjects are treated as static repositories of atomic facts rather than domains of hermeneutic interpretation, aesthetic value, or critical discourse.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Evaluates text-only outputs generated by language models in response to open-ended, fact-seeking prompts.
analytical_tasks_explanation: The benchmark decomposes complex narrative text into binary 'atomic facts' and validates them against search results. It tests literal factual recall (dates, names, events) rather than deeper historical causal analysis, aesthetic critique, or interpretive argumentation characteristic of humanities scholarship.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Spans 38 broad topics across STEM, Social Sciences, Humanities, and other domains.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates 13 generative language models from Gemini, GPT, Claude, and PaLM-2 families.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary focus is testing and ranking the generative capacities of LLMs.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes humanities disciplines such as History, Philosophy, Religious Studies, Music, and Visual/Performing Arts (Movies, Architecture).

quantitative_metrics.note: Utilizes factual precision, recall, and the newly proposed F1@K metric based on automated claims ratings.

published_after_march_2023.note: The preprint was published in March 2024 and formally presented at NeurIPS in November 2024.

english_language.note: The prompt dataset, evaluation system, and scientific paper are presented entirely in English.

summary: This paper presents LongFact, a dataset of 2,280 prompts across 38 topics designed to benchmark the factual accuracy of long-form LLM generations. To scale evaluation, the authors introduce SAFE, an automated agent that deconstructs model responses into atomic facts and verifes them via search engines, and propose F1@K to balance precision and recall. A benchmark of 13 leading LLMs shows that scaling and RLHF significantly improve long-form factuality.
key_findings: The proposed automated evaluator, SAFE, achieves 72% agreement with crowdsourced human annotators and outperforms them on disagreement cases (76% accuracy) at a fraction of the cost. Benchmarking results show that larger models (such as GPT-4-Turbo and Gemini-Ultra) demonstrate superior long-form factuality, primarily driven by improvements in factual recall rather than precision.
